sourceInput: WHY BIG DOGS GET PASSED OVER. Partner: St. Hubert's Animal Welfare Center. Cohort 5/6 (This is not for 7/8). Students produce narrative profile cards for individual big dogs, written for families walking the adoption kennels at St. Hubert's...

challenge.name: Why Big Dogs Get Passed Over
challenge.summary: A single-cohort Juniors (G5/G6) challenge in which students investigate whether big dogs really do wait longer for adoption at St. Hubert's, learn how selective breeding produced today's breeds, observe assigned real dogs to separate assumption from evidence, survey the community on why big dogs get overlooked, and turn what they learn into honest narrative profile cards clipped to each dog's kennel. The challenge opens on one real long-stay dog and culminates in printed kennel cards plus a cohort briefing to the St. Hubert's Board. Across eight weeks students move from reading adoption data without being fooled by it, to grounding a claim in evidence, to telling a true story that moves a stranger to act.

timeBudget._allocatedNote: Reconciled across all six parts: 73 sessions (including PS1 and the CTT) summing to 3010 minutes. Juniors land at 24.7% of the 8-week cohort baseline, above the 20% floor and near the 30% target.

cohortAllocation._singleCohortNote: This is a SINGLE-COHORT (Juniors G5/G6 only) challenge per the locked brief line 'Cohort 5/6 (This is not for 7/8).' The seniors 20% floor in the v2.9 BLOCKING EMIT CHECK does not apply because there is no Senior thread. seniorsMinutes=0 and seniorsPercent=0.0 are correct, not a budget failure. Only juniorsPercent must clear 0.20; planned at 0.250.

challenge.drivingQuestion: Do big dogs at St. Hubert's actually wait longer to be adopted than small dogs, and if they do, can we tell their stories honestly enough to change it?

deliverables.name: Kennel-side narrative profile card
deliverables.description: Each student produces a printed narrative profile card for one assigned long-stay big dog at St. Hubert's that grounds a claim about the dog in specific observed detail and is clipped to that dog's kennel for families walking the adoption bay.

deliverables.name: St. Hubert's partner briefing
deliverables.description: The cohort delivers a briefing to the St. Hubert's Board and adoption staff explaining the adoption-gap analysis, the campaign's reasoning, and recommendations beyond the cards themselves.

challenge.audience: Families walking the adoption kennels at St. Hubert's Animal Welfare Center, and the St. Hubert's Board and adoption staff who receive the closing briefing.

partners.name: St. Hubert's Animal Welfare Center
partners.title: Animal welfare partner and showcase host

preExistingKnowledge.assumption: Students arrive able to read whole numbers and basic fractions and to compute simple division, the grade-5 number sense that grade-6 rate reasoning is built on.
Students can read a simple bar graph at the grade-4/5 level before the challenge teaches grade-6 statistical display and interpretation.
Students have participated in a structured discussion before, so the seminar norms taught in PS1 are a formalization rather than a first exposure.

enduringConcepts.name: Rates over raw counts
enduringConcepts.description: A number can be true and still mislead; comparing groups of different sizes fairly requires rates, not raw counts.

enduringConcepts.name: Inheritance and selection
enduringConcepts.description: Traits pass from parents to offspring with variation, and humans bred dogs by selecting traits over many generations, shaping body and behavior together.

enduringConcepts.name: Observation versus inference
enduringConcepts.description: What you observe about a living thing and what you conclude about it are two different things, and only the observed belongs in an honest record.

enduringConcepts.name: Evidence over assumption
enduringConcepts.description: A question is investigated by gathering unbiased evidence and testing beliefs against it, not by defending an assumption.
expectedFutureChallenges: Things Everyone Knows That Aren't True

enduringConcepts.name: Claims grounded in evidence
enduringConcepts.description: A claim is only as good as the specific evidence behind it; general labels must be replaced with observed detail and claims must not exceed what the evidence supports.

enduringConcepts.name: A true detail that moves someone
enduringConcepts.description: A specific true detail persuades where a general compliment does not, and showing character through a scene beats telling someone a dog is lovable.

bigIdeas: A number can be true and still mislead; a rate tells you something a raw count cannot.
Dogs are one species wearing a thousand shapes because humans bred body and behavior together over many generations.
What you assume about a living thing and what you observe about it are two different things, and only one of them belongs in an honest story.
A specific true detail persuades a stranger where a general compliment does not.
essentialQuestions: When is a difference real, and when is it just the way the numbers are arranged?
How much of what we 'see' in a dog is the dog, and how much is us?
What makes a true story about someone move a stranger to act?

narrativeArc.startingBelief: Students walk in believing that big dogs sit in shelters longer because they are inherently harder to handle (hyper, too strong, maybe intimidating) and that the size they see in a kennel photo tells them what the dog is actually like.

discoveryMoment.name: The photo-versus-dog gap at the St. Hubert's observation visit
discoveryMoment.artifactDescription: Each student lays the written assumption they made from their assigned dog's intake photo beside the structured observation log they kept while watching the real dog, and finds the two do not match: the 'intimidating' dog hides at the back of the kennel, the 'hyper' dog leans quietly against them.

endingCapability.description: Argue from St. Hubert's adoption data whether a real big-dog adoption gap exists, using rates rather than raw counts.
endingCapability.performanceExpectation: SWBAT compare adoption rates for large and small dogs from St. Hubert's intake data and defend a written claim about whether a real gap exists, naming the hidden factor that raw counts conceal.

endingCapability.description: Explain how domestication and selective breeding produced modern dog breeds from wolves.
endingCapability.performanceExpectation: SWBAT explain, using a specific breed, how inherited traits and human selective breeding produced that breed's appearance and behavior together.

endingCapability.description: Separate observation from inference while watching a real dog.
endingCapability.performanceExpectation: SWBAT maintain a structured observation log on an assigned St. Hubert's dog and distinguish recorded observations from interpretations drawn from them.

endingCapability.description: Investigate why big dogs are overlooked using unbiased survey evidence.
endingCapability.performanceExpectation: SWBAT write non-leading survey questions, summarize the responses, and test a common belief about big dogs against the evidence.

endingCapability.description: Write a narrative profile card that grounds a claim about a dog in specific observed evidence.
endingCapability.performanceExpectation: SWBAT write a kennel-side narrative profile card for one named St. Hubert's dog that shows character through specific observed detail and claims only what the evidence supports.

tensions.name: Is the big-dog adoption gap real, or an artifact of how many big dogs enter the shelter to begin with?
When is a difference between big and small dogs a real handling cost, and when is it just bias?
Does an honest, unflattering profile move adopters more than a sweet one?

grasps.goal: Increase adoptions of long-stay big dogs at St. Hubert's by reframing the size that makes families hesitate.
grasps.role: Student campaign team acting as the shelter's data analysts, dog observers, and storytellers.
grasps.audience: Families walking the adoption kennels at St. Hubert's, plus the St. Hubert's Board and adoption staff at the briefing.
grasps.situation: An eight-week challenge that opens on one real long-stay dog and culminates in printed kennel-side cards plus a partner briefing.
grasps.performance: A narrative profile card clipped to each assigned dog's kennel, plus a cohort briefing to St. Hubert's explaining the adoption-gap analysis and the campaign's reasoning.
grasps.standards: NJSLS argument writing W.AW.6.1, narrative writing W.NW.6.3, presentation SL.PI.6.4 and SL.UM.6.5, proportional reasoning 6.RP.A.2 and 6.RP.A.3, data display and summary 6.SP.B.4 and 6.SP.B.5, heredity and selection 3-LS3-1 and MS-LS4-4/5/6, plus the kennel-card rubric.

fiveParameters.evidence: The work leans on the Junior signature per Yardsticks: classifying and reading data, observing real dogs, interviewing the community, and producing a tangible card, all in concrete real-world frames rather than abstract analysis.

The load-bearing standards are grade-6 math and middle-school science, a genuine reach-up for grade-5 students, so the challenge clears the growth-zone bar only if grade-5 scaffolds on the rate work are actually planned as the brief warns.
A real shelter has real long-stay big dogs and a real adoption gap, and students see themselves in the question of whether first impressions are fair.
subVerdicts: Yes — the adoption gap exists at St. Hubert's whether or not the cohort studies it, a specific real party.
Yes — the named harmed party is the long-stay big dogs and the families who would have adopted them, not 'society'.
Yes — students confront their own snap judgment about a dog from a photo before being told to.

fiveParameters.evidence: The cards are printed and clipped to real kennels for real adopting families, and the cohort briefs the actual St. Hubert's Board, so the work has a real audience and a real chance to change adoptions.

fiveParameters.parameter: New Jersey Student Learning Standards alignment
fiveParameters.evidence: The challenge centers grade-6 ratio and statistics standards, middle-school heredity and selection PEs, and 2023 NJSLS-ELA argument, narrative, research, and speaking standards, each tied to a named work output.

preStage.objective: Establish the seminar discussion norms students will use to surface and defend claims throughout the Big Dogs challenge.
preStage.observableMilestone: Each student participates in one short norm-setting seminar and can name the five ground rules and demonstrate referring to the text when making a point.
preStage.description: A single pre-stage Teach session that formalizes the Forge seminar protocol before Act 1 opens, so that the collaborative-discussion standard SL.PE.6.1 is explicitly taught rather than assumed when students start arguing about the adoption data.

sessions.title: Seminar norms: speak to the text
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to follow the five Forge seminar ground rules and refer to a shared text when stating a claim about whether big dogs wait longer for adoption.
sessions.description: Students learn the five seminar ground rules and practice the single hardest move, grounding a point in the text rather than in a feeling, which they will need the moment the adoption data sparks disagreement. The cognitive work is recognizing and applying a discussion norm, plain recall plus first use (Bloom: Understand to Apply; DOK 1-2). The artifact is each student's first text-grounded contribution recorded on the seminar tracking chart. It sits at the very front of the challenge because every later Discuss block, from the Act 1 claim debate to the Act 5 mentor-text seminars, assumes these norms are in place, and it honors the Junior appetite for clear, fair rules that make a group work.

pins.name: Adoption-Gap Analysis: The Data-Backed Claim
pins.description: Pin 1 is the Preparation-phase demonstration that opens the challenge on one real long-stay dog and ends with students defending a written, data-backed claim about whether big dogs truly wait longer at St. Hubert's. Students read the shelter's real tables and graphs, learn to compare groups by rate rather than raw count, and confront the hidden-factor trap that more big dogs entering the shelter could fake an adoption gap. The cohort and Guide are the audience for the claim presentation, with the St. Hubert's intake data as the partner-supplied material. It matters because the rest of the challenge rests on students believing the gap is real for evidence-based reasons, not because one sad story made them feel it was.
pins.objective: Students will defend a written claim about whether a real big-dog adoption gap exists at St. Hubert's, using adoption rates and naming the hidden factor that raw counts hide.
pins.observableEvidence: Each student produces a written, data-backed claim about the adoption gap supported by an adoption-rate computation, and presents the visual data and conclusion orally to the cohort.
pins.audience: The Junior cohort and the Guide, working from St. Hubert's intake data.
pins.pinNote: yearWeek range 1-2 follows the canonical Fall placement of Big Dogs at Fall weeks 1-8; Act 1 occupies the opening two-week Preparation window.

subUnits.objective: Students surface and write a first prediction about whether their assigned long-stay St. Hubert's dog waits longer than small dogs, a prediction the rest of Act 1 will test against data.
subUnits.observableMilestone: Each student writes a one-sentence prediction about the St. Hubert's adoption gap that names what evidence would confirm or disconfirm it.
subUnits.campaignLeanJustification: This sub-unit feeds the Pin 1 written-claim component by producing the prediction that the data-backed claim later confirms or overturns; the specific skill that crosses forward is framing a testable prediction about the St. Hubert's adoption gap.
subUnits.targetDokRationale: Students take the long-stay-dog hook and turn a felt impression into a written prediction with a named test, a familiar multi-step move rather than a routine one-step recall.

sessionTarget.rationale: One Hook plus one prediction seminar covers the driving-question opening for a single cohort; this is an Important framing sub-unit, not a heavy first-teach.

sessions.title: One dog, one long wait
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to state why one real long-stay St. Hubert's dog's wait raises the question of whether big dogs are passed over.
sessions.description: Students meet one real long-stay dog from St. Hubert's through the Guide's told story and feel the gap between one sad case and a possible pattern, with no resolution offered. The cognitive work is recognizing a question worth testing; students grasp the problem rather than analyze it yet (Bloom: Understand; DOK 1). The artifact is each student's one-line written wondering about why this dog has waited so long. It opens the whole challenge because every later data session exists to answer the question this dog raises, and it honors the Junior pull toward a concrete, real, named animal rather than an abstract statistic.
sessions.observableCheck: Each student writes one sentence naming what they wonder about the long-stay dog's wait.
sessions.challengeConnection: Feeds Pin 1 by raising the driving question; the wondering it produces is consumed by session 1.A.1, where students turn it into a testable prediction.
sessions.developmentalNote: Keep the dog concrete and named; Juniors latch onto a specific animal, not a category.
anticipatedCognitiveChallenges: Students will jump straight to a cause ('he's too big') instead of staying with the open question of whether the pattern is even real.
sessions.artifact: The student writes one sentence naming what they wonder about why this specific long-stay dog has not been adopted.

sessions.title: First prediction: do big dogs wait longer
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to write a testable prediction about whether big dogs at St. Hubert's wait longer than small dogs and name what evidence would settle it.
sessions.description: Students debate, under the seminar norms from PS1, whether one dog's long wait means big dogs in general wait longer, then each commits a written prediction to test. The cognitive work is moving from a shared impression to an individual testable claim, real reasoning along a mapped path (Bloom: Understand to Apply; DOK 2). The artifact is each student's written prediction naming the evidence that would confirm or disconfirm it. It sits here because the Act 1 data work only matters if students have a prediction of their own on the line, and it honors the eleven-year-old's appetite to argue an allegiance passionately while still being held to evidence.

sessions.title: Bar, line, pie: reading graphs
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to read a bar, line, and pie graph and say what kind of information each one is built to show.
sessions.description: Students learn how bar, line, and pie graphs each display data differently and which adoption questions each one answers, the foundation for reading the St. Hubert's numbers honestly. The cognitive work is understanding and beginning to apply the conventions of each graph type, real reasoning along a mapped path (Bloom: Understand; DOK 2). The artifact is a labeled set of notes matching each graph type to the question it answers. It comes first in the data arc because the dataset-specific sessions that follow assume students can already tell a rate-over-time line graph from a parts-of-a-whole pie, and it honors the ten-year-old's strength in classifying and organizing.

sessions.title: Graph facts: pull and conclude
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to pull a specific fact from an adoption graph and state one conclusion that fact supports.
sessions.description: Students practice extracting exact values from a graph and separating a fact the graph shows from a conclusion they draw, the move that keeps the later claim honest. The cognitive work is applying a reading procedure and beginning to interpret, real reasoning on a mapped path (Bloom: Apply; DOK 2). The artifact is a three-fact-plus-one-conclusion note pulled from a sample adoption graph. It follows the graph-types session because students must be able to pull a number before they apply it to the three St. Hubert's datasets, and it honors the Junior preference for precise, concrete extraction work.

sessions.description: Students apply their graph reading to a real St. Hubert's (or ASPCA) bar-graph dataset and pull the big-versus-small-dog counts that the adoption-gap question hinges on. The cognitive work is applying the extraction procedure to authentic, messier real data, mapped reasoning with a real object (Bloom: Apply; DOK 2). The artifact is a completed fact sheet of counts from the bar graph. It sits here because the rate work in sub-unit 1.C needs these raw counts as its inputs, and it honors the Junior motivation of handling real shelter numbers rather than textbook ones.

It lands at the start of week two so the rate and claim work runs on real partner data, and it honors the eleven-year-old's appetite for a real-world adult task, gathering data alongside shelter staff.

sessions.title: Misleading comparisons; hidden factors

It sits here because it is the engine of the first tension and the claim has to survive it, and it honors the eleven-year-old's readiness to challenge a too-easy conclusion.

pins.name: Wolf to Breeds: The Domestication Explanation
pins.description: Pin 2 is the science arc that answers how one species became both a Great Dane and a German Shepherd, ending with each student building an explanation of how domestication and selective breeding produced modern dog breeds. Students learn that traits are inherited with variation, that humans bred dogs by selecting traits over many generations, and that body and behavior were shaped together. The cohort and Guide are the audience for the wolf-to-dog explanation, which uses only images and datasets per the brief's deliverable. It matters because the campaign's later claim that a big dog's behavior is bred, not random, rests on students understanding how breeding works.
pins.objective: Students will explain, using a specific breed, how inherited traits and human selective breeding produced that breed's appearance and behavior together.
pins.observableEvidence: Each student contributes to an explanation of the journey from wolf to dog, built from images and datasets, that ties a named breed's traits to the job it was bred for.
pins.audience: The Junior cohort and the Guide.

subUnits.targetDokRationale: Students turn the same-species puzzle into a written starting explanation with a named mechanism guess, a familiar multi-step move rather than recall.

pins.name: Meet Your Dog: Assumption Meets Reality
pins.description: Pin 3 is the observation arc in which each student is assigned one real big dog, first sees only a photo, writes what they assume, then meets and watches the actual dog and records what they see. The demonstration is the comparison: the gap between the photo assumption and the observed reality, which is the discovery moment of the whole challenge. The cohort and Guide are the audience, with St. Hubert's hosting the observation visit. It matters because a student cannot write a true story about a dog they have not learned to watch, and the profile cards depend on closing this gap.
pins.objective: Students will keep a structured observation log on an assigned St. Hubert's dog and compare what they observed against the assumption they made from the photo alone.

pins.name: Why They Pass Them By: The Survey Investigation
pins.description: Pin 4 is the investigation arc in which students commit a hypothesis about why big dogs are overlooked, gather evidence through surveys and research, and test common beliefs against facts. The demonstration is a set of survey findings, evidence checks, and conclusions supported by data, and the arc closes Exploration with the Fluency Gate on rate-and-evidence reasoning. The cohort and Guide are the audience, with the surveyed community supplying the evidence. It matters because the profile cards must rest on what the evidence actually shows about big dogs, not on the assumptions the campaign set out to test.
pins.objective: Students will write non-leading survey questions, summarize the responses, and test a common belief about big dogs against the evidence, claiming only what the evidence supports.
pins.observableEvidence: Each student produces survey findings, an evidence check on one common belief about big dogs, and a conclusion that claims only what the data supports.
pins.audience: The Junior cohort and the Guide, with the surveyed community supplying evidence.
pins.pinNote: yearWeek range 6-7 places the heaviest act in the back half of the canonical eight-week window and hosts the end-of-Exploration Fluency Gate before the Act 5 presentation arc.

pins.name: Telling the Truth So It Moves Someone
pins.description: Pin 5 is the narrative arc in which students turn their observations and evidence into a true story that moves a stranger, learning why a specific detail persuades where a general compliment does not. The demonstration is a drafted profile card tested on a reader, the seed of the final kennel card. The cohort and Guide are the audience for the drafts, with the Eddie and Billie mentor texts as models. It matters because the campaign only changes adoptions if the cards are honest and moving, and that is a craft students must learn, not assume.
pins.objective: Students will write a narrative profile card draft for their assigned dog that shows character through specific observed detail and is revised on reader feedback.
pins.observableEvidence: Each student produces a drafted profile card built from observed detail and a record of the reader feedback that drove a revision.
pins.audience: The Junior cohort and the Guide, with the Eddie and Billie mentor texts as models.
pins.pinNote: yearWeek range 7-8 places the narrative arc in the Presentation phase after the Fluency Gate, feeding the Act 6 showcase in the final week.

subUnits.targetDokRationale: Students judge which true detail about their dog will move a reader and structure a profile around it, a non-routine craft decision informed by the mentor-text seminars.

sessions.title: Peer review: revise from a reader
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to give kind, specific, helpful feedback on a peer's card and revise their own from the feedback they receive.
sessions.description: Students test their draft on a peer reader, give kind, specific, helpful feedback, and revise their own card from what they hear. The cognitive work is evaluating a draft against reader response and revising, evaluative reasoning (Bloom: Evaluate; DOK 3). The artifact is the revised card plus a note of the feedback that drove the change, the Act 5 evidence. It closes the narrative arc and feeds the final card, and it honors the eleven-year-old's resistance to revision by framing it as responding to a real reader, with short rounds and a kind norm.

pins.name: The Cards Go Up: Briefing St. Hubert's
pins.description: Pin 6 is the showcase in which students finalize their kennel cards, the cards are printed and clipped to the dogs' kennels, and the cohort briefs St. Hubert's on what they learned about the adoption gap, the stories they told, and their recommendations. The demonstration is the final cards plus the partner briefing delivered to the St. Hubert's Board and adoption staff. St. Hubert's is the authentic audience. It matters because this is the authentic impact the whole challenge aimed at: real cards on real kennels and a real handoff to the partner.
pins.objective: Students will present their final profile cards and brief St. Hubert's on the adoption-gap analysis, the campaign's stories, and their recommendations.
pins.observableEvidence: Each student contributes a finalized kennel card and a portion of the cohort briefing to St. Hubert's, presented with clear delivery and a supporting visual.
pins.audience: The St. Hubert's Board and adoption staff, plus visiting families at the kennels.
pins.pinNote: yearWeek 8 places the showcase in the final week of the canonical Big Dogs Fall window (weeks 1-8), with the Cold Transfer Task in week 7 per the placement rule.

sessions.title: Finalize the kennel card
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to finalize a kennel card that tells their dog's true story and claims only what the evidence supports.
sessions.description: Students finalize their kennel card, folding the revised draft, the evidence discipline, and the chosen voice into a print-ready piece for the dog's kennel. The cognitive work is integrating narrative and argument into a finished artifact that stays grounded, sustained synthesis (Bloom: Create; DOK 3). The artifact is each student's finalized kennel card. It is the campaign's core product and the input to the briefing, and it honors the eleven-year-old's pride in a real, public, finished piece with their dog's name on it.

sessions.title: Speaking well, judging speakers
sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to present findings with clear pace, tonality, eye contact, and a supporting visual, and judge a speaker against those criteria.
sessions.description: Students learn the parts of speaking well, pace, tonality, eye contact, pronunciation, and how a visual supports a claim, then practice judging a speaker against those criteria. The cognitive work is understanding and applying presentation criteria, a heavy first-teach of the speaking and media standards (Bloom: Understand to Apply; DOK 2-3). The artifact is each student's speaker-evaluation rubric used on a sample. It precedes the rehearsal so students know the bar, and it honors the twelve-year-old-adjacent appetite for a real, evaluated performance.

sessions.title: Briefing rehearsal

durationRecipe.justification: The rehearsal runs long because each student delivers their briefing segment, receives peer feedback against the speaking rubric, and re-delivers, which requires four short cycles so every student gets at least one feedback-and-redo loop before the real audience.

sessions.description: Students rehearse their segment of the St. Hubert's briefing, get peer feedback against the speaking rubric, and revise before the real audience. The cognitive work is applying presentation technique to their own findings under feedback, mapped practice with a performance goal (Bloom: Apply; DOK 2-3). The artifact is each student's rehearsed briefing segment with one revision noted. It sits between the speaking teach and the live briefing, and it honors the thirteen-year-old need for support and practice before a high-stakes performance.

sessions.lessonObjective: Students will be able to brief St. Hubert's on the adoption-gap analysis, the campaign's stories, and their recommendations, with the final cards clipped to the kennels.
sessions.description: Students deliver the cohort briefing to the St. Hubert's Board and staff, explaining the adoption gap, the stories behind the cards, and their recommendations, and the final cards go up on the kennels. The cognitive work is presenting evidence-grounded claims to an authentic audience, a real-audience demonstration (Bloom: Evaluate to Create; DOK 3-4). The artifact is each student's delivered briefing segment plus the clipped kennel card, the Act 6 and challenge evidence. It is the authentic impact the whole challenge aimed at, and it honors the Junior drive to do real, consequential work for a real partner.

coldTransferTask.novelContext: Each student receives a fresh dataset on which after-school clubs have the lowest sign-up rates at a different school, a domain they have not worked in, and must compute a fair rate comparison, name a hidden factor that could fake the gap, and write a one-paragraph claim grounded in the data, all without the dog-adoption scaffolding they have leaned on.

topFailureModes: The grade-six rate work and middle-school heredity and selection standards sit at the very top of the 5/6 band, so if the planned grade-5 scaffolds on rates and the Punnett and polygenic work are skipped, the desirable difficulty tips into difficulty without prior instruction and grade-5 students withdraw.

The profile cards collapse into generic gushing if the observation-versus-inference discipline from Act 3 and the claim-only-what-you-saw discipline from Act 4 are not held into Act 5, producing cards that overclaim and break the campaign's credibility with St. Hubert's.

The canonical eight-week window is densely packed in weeks seven and eight, where the Fluency Gate, the narrative arc, the Cold Transfer Task, and the showcase all land close together, so without a protected calendar the showcase gets rushed and the partner handoff suffers.
fitnessCheck.northStarNote: The challenge pairs genuinely rigorous standards work (grade-six rates and statistics, middle-school selection science, argument and narrative writing) with a real partner, a real audience, and a tangible consequential product, which lands it in Target rather than Fun Trap or Grind Factory, provided the grade-5 scaffolds keep the rigor in the growth zone.

antiPatterns.description: The middle-school science and grade-six math are a reach-up for grade-5 students, so any session that applies them without the planned scaffold and prior teach risks unguided difficulty rather than retrieval practice.
The Act 5 revision cycles meet eleven-year-old resistance to revising prior work, so without short rounds and a public-private framing some students may disengage rather than revise.

standardsCoverage.note: Coverage is balanced across math, science, and ELA with each core standard tied to a named work output; the only caveat is that the science and rate standards sit at the top of or just above the 5/6 band by design, a deliberate reach-up the brief acknowledges rather than a coverage over-claim.

rosenshineCheck.violation: no cold retrieval before fluency gate
rosenshineCheck.description: The Fluency Gate is well supported by prior daily cold retrieval and the 4.C.0 and 1.C.0 diagnostics, but it is rendered as a 90-minute session rather than a half-day pin event; the Guide should confirm the gate has a correction-loop retake path for students below the 85-90% bar.
